Transit Access Coverage Dashboard — Rockingham County, NC

CR2C2 Southeast Data Competition 2026  ·  NC A&T State University  ·  Dr. Venktesh Pandey

What This Dashboard Shows

This dashboard visualizes transit accessibility across Rockingham County, NC by measuring how long residents must walk to reach the nearest SKAT bus stop. The county is divided into 2,195 H3 hexagonal zones (~0.74 km² each), with population areal-weighted from ACS 2023 block group estimates.

Walk times are computed as straight-line (haversine) distance from each hexagon centroid to the nearest of the 67 SKAT stop locations, converted at 1.4 m/s (FTA standard walking speed). Each stop is also scored on a vulnerability index combining population, poverty rate, and elderly share — identifying where transit improvements would have the greatest equity impact.

🚶 Walk Time Analysis

Hexagons colored by 6 walk time bands (0–5 min to 45+ min). 42.2% of residents (38,368) face a walk exceeding 45 minutes to the nearest stop — a de facto transit desert.

● Stop Vulnerability Index

Each SKAT stop scored 0–100 using equal weights: population served, poverty rate, and elderly share. Stops colored dark navy (high need) to light blue (lower need). Click any stop for full details.

★ Triad-Danville Connector

The county's only intercity transit stop at Reidsville Walmart. A 280-minute evening gap between the last SKAT departure (3:15 PM) and the southbound Connector arrival (7:55 PM) is the critical schedule coordination gap identified in this study.

--- SKAT ¼-Mile Walkshed

The combined ¼-mile (402m) buffer around all 67 stops per FTA guidelines. Toggle layers and filter stops by city or name using the sidebar controls in the Map View.

How to Use This Dashboard

→ Click Map View to explore the interactive map
→ Click any hexagon to see population + walk time
→ Click any stop dot to see vulnerability score
→ Click Key Findings for the full analysis
→ Use Color Hexagons By to switch data layers
→ Use Layer Controls to toggle map elements

Data: ACS 2023 · SKAT GTFS (ITRE/NC State, v1) · Census TIGER 2023 · H3 res-8 · Haversine distance · 1.4 m/s FTA walk speed

Legend
ACS 2023 · SKAT GTFS (ITRE v1) · Census TIGER 2023
H3 res-8 hexagons · Haversine · 1.4 m/s FTA

What This Analysis Shows

This dashboard measures how long residents of Rockingham County would need to walk to reach the nearest SKAT bus stop — computed as straight-line (haversine) distance from each hexagonal zone centroid to the nearest of the 67 SKAT stop locations, converted to walk time at 1.4 m/s (FTA standard walking speed).

The county is divided into 2,195 H3 hexagonal zones (resolution 8, ~0.74 km² each), with population areal-weighted from ACS 2023 block group estimates. This provides a uniform, unbiased spatial framework — unlike block groups, hexagons do not vary in size, avoiding the visual distortion caused by large rural polygons dominating the map.

Each SKAT stop is also scored for vulnerability using a composite index of population, poverty rate, and elderly share — helping identify which stops serve the most at-risk communities and where new routes or mobile services would have the greatest equity impact.

Walk Time Distribution

🟢 Within 10 Minutes — 24.8% of Population

22,506 residents live within a 10-minute walk of a SKAT stop (0–5 min: 9,969 · 5–10 min: 12,537). These are concentrated in the dense stop clusters of Eden/Leaksville, Reidsville core, and Mayodan town center. For these residents, SKAT is genuinely walkable.

🟡 10–30 Minutes — 21.8% of Population

19,848 residents face a 10–30 minute walk — at the outer edge of practical walkability. At 1.4 m/s, 20 minutes means roughly 1.7 km (just over a mile). In a rural county without footpaths or sidewalks, this distance is effectively inaccessible for elderly residents and those with mobility limitations.

🔴 Over 30 Minutes — 53.5% of Population

48,616 residents — more than half the county — face a walk exceeding 30 minutes to the nearest SKAT stop. Of these, 38,368 residents (42.2%) face a walk of 45 minutes or more, representing a de facto transit desert where SKAT is inaccessible without private transport.

Stop Vulnerability Ranking

Each of the 67 SKAT stops is scored on a 0–100 vulnerability index using equal weights across three dimensions:

Population (⅓): Total residents within ½-mile catchment — higher population means greater potential impact.
Poverty Rate (⅓): % of catchment population below poverty line — higher poverty signals greater car-free dependency.
Elderly Share (⅓): % aged 65+ — higher elderly share signals greater mobility limitations and healthcare trip demand.

Equal weights are used absent empirical mode-choice data for Rockingham County — consistent with the precautionary principle in composite vulnerability indices (cf. FEMA SVI methodology).

#1 RHS Apartments (score 67) leads due to high combined population, poverty, and elderly share in its catchment. Kathy's Mart and Washington St/Downtown Leaksville rank high on poverty (54.8%) despite smaller populations. Click any stop on the map to see its full profile.

Connection to Schedule Misalignment

Walk time barriers compound the schedule misalignment at the Reidsville Walmart — the county's only Triad-Danville Connector stop. For the 53.5% of residents facing a 30+ minute walk to any SKAT stop:

Step 1: They cannot walk to SKAT — they need RCATS (3-day advance booking) or Local Link (Eden/Mayodan zones only, 7:30 AM–3:30 PM) just to reach Reidsville Walmart.

Step 2: Once at Reidsville Walmart, a 280-minute evening gap exists — last SKAT departs 3:15 PM, Connector southbound arrives 7:55 PM.

The result: a trip to Greensboro or Danville requires navigating three separate booking systems, two transfers, and a 4+ hour wait — all for a $4–$8 ticket.
